honest note: this guide assumes you already know basic C syntax (variables, loops, if/else, functions). It starts “from scratch” on theDSA-specificparts of C — pointers, structs, dynamic memory — because that’s where most people’s foundations are actually shaky, and everything after depends on being solid there. 1 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide Part 0: C Essentials for DSA Almost every DSA bug you’ll hit in C traces back to a shaky grip on one of these four things. Get comfortable here and the rest of this guide gets a lot easier. 0.1 Pointers (the single most important concept in this guide) A variable is a labeled box that holds a value. A pointer is a labeled box that holds anaddress— the location ofanotherbox in memory. intx=10; int*p=&x;// p now holds the ADDRESS of x, not the value 10 printf("%d
",x);// 10-> the value printf("%p
",&x);// 0x7ffee...-> the address of x printf("%p
",p);// 0x7ffee...-> same address, because p points to x printf("%d
",*p);// 10-> "dereference" p: go to that address, get the value Two operators to internalize: •&x— “address of x”

of x printf("%p\n",p);// 0x7ffee...-> same address, because p points to x printf("%d\n",*p);// 10-> "dereference" p: go to that address, get the value Two operators to internalize: •&x— “address of x” (turns a value into a pointer) •*p— “value at the address p points to” (turns a pointer back into a value) Why DSA cares so much about pointers:every linked structure (linked lists, trees, graphs) is built from nodes connected by pointers. Without pointers, you can’t build anything that isn’t a fixed-size array. Pointers to pointersshow up constantly when a function needs to modify a pointer itself (not just what it points to) — e.g., inserting a node at the head of a linked list needs to change what theheadvariable points to, frominsidea function: voidupdateHead(structNode**headRef){ // headRef is the address of the head pointer itself *headRef=someNewNode;// this changes the actual head variable in the caller } This trips up almost everyone the first time. The rule of thumb:ifafunctionneedstopermanentlychange what a pointer points to, it needs a pointer to that pointer. 0.2 Arrays and memory An array is just a contiguous block

The rule of thumb:ifafunctionneedstopermanentlychange what a pointer points to, it needs a pointer to that pointer. 0.2 Arrays and memory An array is just a contiguous block of memory.arr[i]is really shorthand for*(arr + i)— “goisteps past the start address and read the value there.” This is why array indices start at 0:arr[0]is*(arr + 0), zero steps from the start. intarr[5]={10,20,30,40,50}; printf("%d\n",arr[2]);// 30 printf("%d\n",*(arr+2));// 30 — identical An array name, when used in most expressions, “decays” into a pointer to its first element. This is why you can pass arrays to functions using a pointer parameter: 2 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide voidprintArray(int*arr,intsize){// same as int arr[] for(inti=0;i<size;i++) printf("%d",arr[i]); } Important gotcha:because arrays decay to pointers when passed to functions,sizeof(arr)insidea func- tion that received the array as a parameter will NOT give you the array’s size — it gives you the size of a pointer (usually 8 bytes). Always pass the size explicitly as a separate parameter. 0.3 Structs — building your own data types A struct groups related variables under one name. This

This is how every custom data structure in this guide gets built. structPoint{ intx; inty; }; structPoint p1={3,4}; printf("%d,%d\n",p1.x,p1.y);// dot notation for direct access structPoint*ptr=&p1; printf("%d\n",ptr->x);// arrow notation when accessing through a pointer // ptr->x is shorthand for (*ptr).x To avoid writingstruct Pointeverywhere, most DSA code usestypedef: typedefstructNode{ intdata; structNode*next; }Node; Node n;// instead of struct Node n; Throughout this guide, structs are written explicitly asstruct Nodefor clarity about what’s happening, but you’ll seetypedef’d versions everywhere in the wild — they’re identical underneath. 0.4 Dynamic memory allocation Arrays declared normally (int arr[10]) have a fixed size decided at compile time. Most real data structures need to grow and shrink at runtime — that needs the heap. are garbage (uninitialized) calloc(n, size)Allocatesnblocks ofsizebytes each, zeroed out realloc(ptr, newSize)Resizes a previous allocation, preserving existing contents 3 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide FunctionWhat it does free(ptr) Releases memory back to the system structNode*newNode=(structNode*)malloc(sizeof(structNode)); if(newNode==NULL){ // malloc failed — always check this in real code exit(1); } newNode->data=5; newNode->next=NULL; // ... use it ... free(newNode);// give the memory back when you're done The golden rule: everymallocneeds exactly one matchingfree.Forget to free and you get amem- ory leak(memory that’s lost but never released). Free twice, or use memory after freeing it, and you get undefined behavior— often a crash, sometimes silent corruption that shows up much later. In interviews, casually mentioning that you’d free allocated nodes (even if you don’t write every free call) signals that you understand ownership — interviewers notice this. 0.5 Recursion basics A recursive function calls itself on a smaller version of the same problem, until it hits abase casesimple enough to answer directly. intfactorial(intn){ if(n<=1)return1;// base case — stops the recursion returnn*factorial(n-1);// recursive case — smaller subproblem } Every recursive call gets

same problem, until it hits abase casesimple enough to answer directly. intfactorial(intn){ if(n<=1)return1;// base case — stops the recursion returnn*factorial(n-1);// recursive case — smaller subproblem } Every recursive call gets pushed onto thecall stack. Callingfactorial(5)looks like this internally: factorial(5) -> 5 * factorial(4) -> 4 * factorial(3) -> 3 * factorial(2) -> 2 * factorial(1) -> returns 1 (base case hit) <- returns 2 * 1 = 2 <- returns 3 * 2 = 6 <- returns 4 * 6 = 24 <- returns 5 * 24 = 120 Two things to always check when writing recursion: 1.Does every path eventually reach the base case? If not, infinite recursion → stack overflow (a crash). 2.Is the problem actually getting smaller each call? (n - 1, half the array, etc.) 4 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide Recursion will come back heavily in Part 6, but this mental model — “smaller subproblem + base case + trust that the recursive call works” — is the foundation for everything from tree traversals to dynamic programming. Interview Radar for Part 0:Interviewers rarely ask about pointers/structs directly, but they’ll absolutely notice if you fumble&,*, or double pointers while coding a linked list problem live. This section is founda- tion, not filler — most “silly mistakes” in DSA interviews are actually pointer mistakes. 5 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide Part 1: Complexity Analysis 1.1 Why this matters more than the code itself In an interview, writingcorrectcode is the minimum bar. What separates a good answer from a great one is whether you can say, unprompted:“this is O(n log n) time and O(n) space, and here’s why.”Interviewers are evaluating whether you understand the cost of what you wrote, not just whether it compiles. 1.2 Big O notation, explained simply Big O describes how theruntime or memoryof an algorithm grows as the input size (n) grows — not the exact number of operations, just theshapeof the growth curve. It answers:

Big O describes how theruntime or memoryof an algorithm grows as the input size (n) grows — not the exact number of operations, just theshapeof the growth curve. It answers: “if I double the input, roughly what happens to the work?” NotationNameIf you doublen...Example O(1)ConstantWork stays the sameAccessingarr[5] O(log n)LogarithmicWork barely increases Binary search O(n)LinearWork doublesLooping through an array once O(n log n)LinearithmicWork slightly more than doubles Merge sort, quick sort O(n²)QuadraticWork quadruplesNested loops over the same array O(2ⁿ)ExponentialWork squaresNaive recursive Fibonacci O(n!)FactorialExplodesBrute-force permutations Here’s that table as a growth picture — the gap between these classes gets brutal fast: Operations ^ |O(n!) |O(2^n) |O(n^2) |O(n log n) |O(n) |O(log n) | O(1) +--------------------------------------------------> n (input size) Atn = 20: O(log n) ≈ 4 operations. O(n) = 20. O(n²) = 400. O(2ⁿ) ≈ 1,000,000. That last jump is why “can you avoid the exponential approach” is often the entire point of a DP question (more in Part 7). 1.3 How to actually read code for complexity Rule 1 — sequential statements add: 6 Data Structures & Algorithms in C —

O(2n), O(n + 5), and O(n) are all just written as O(n). Rule 2 — nested loops multiply: for(inti=0;i<n;i++){ for(intj=0;j<n;j++){...}// inner loop runs n times, for each of n outer iterations↪ } // Total: O(n * n) = O(n^2) Rule 3 — a loop that halves the problem each time is O(log n): while(n>1){ n=n/2;// how many times can you halve n before it hits 1? log2(n) times. } Rule 4 — recursive calls: count total calls, not just depth.A recursive function that makes 2 calls each shrinkingnby 1 (like naive Fibonacci) branches into roughly 2ⁿ total calls — that’s where O(2ⁿ) comes from. A recursive function that makes 1 call shrinkingnby half (like binary search) makes

shrinkingnby 1 (like naive Fibonacci) branches into roughly 2ⁿ total calls — that’s where O(2ⁿ) comes from. A recursive function that makes 1 call shrinkingnby half (like binary search) makes only O(log n) calls. 1.4 Best, Average, and Worst case The same algorithm can have different complexities depending on the input. Quick sort, for instance: •Best/Average case:O(n log n) — the pivot splits the array roughly evenly •Worst case:O(n²) — the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (e.g., an already-sorted array with a naive pivot choice) When someone says “Big O,” they almost always meanworst caseunless stated otherwise — that’s the guarantee you can rely on. Interviewers care about all three, but worst case is the default when you just say “the complexity is...”. 1.5 Space complexity Same idea, but for memory instead of time — how muchextramemory does the algorithm use beyond the input itself, as a function ofn? intsumArray(intarr[],intn){ intsum=0;// O(1) extra space — one variable, regardless of n for(inti=0;i<n;i++) sum+=arr[i]; returnsum; } int*doubleArray(intarr[],intn){ 7 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide int*result=malloc(n*sizeof(int));// O(n)

function ofn? intsumArray(intarr[],intn){ intsum=0;// O(1) extra space — one variable, regardless of n for(inti=0;i<n;i++) sum+=arr[i]; returnsum; } int*doubleArray(intarr[],intn){ 7 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide int*result=malloc(n*sizeof(int));// O(n) extra space — grows with input for(inti=0;i<n;i++) result[i]=arr[i]*2; returnresult; } Don’t forget thecall stackcounts as space too — a recursive function with depthnuses O(n) space even if it allocates no extra variables, because each pending call sits on the stack. Interview Radar for Part 1:You will be asked “what’s the time complexity?” after nearly every single problem you solve, without fail. Practice saying it out loud, unprompted, the moment you finish writing code — don’t wait to be asked. If you can also state the space complexity and mention whether the time/space could be traded off against each other, that’s a strong signal. 8 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide Part 2: Linear Data Structures Linear data structures store elements in a sequence, one after another. This null terminator is the single most important thing to remember about C strings; forget it and every string function reads into garbage memory. charname[20]="Likith"; // In memory: 'L' 'i' 'k' 'i' 't' 'h' '\0' ??? ??? ... //the '\0' marks where the actual string ends Common library functions (#include <string.h>): FunctionDoes strlen(s)Length, not counting\0— O(n) strcpy(dest, src)Copy src into dest strcat(dest, src)Append src onto the end of dest strcmp(a, b)0 if equal, <0 if a < b, >0 if a > b strchr(s, c)Find first occurrence of char c A classic interview exercise is implementing these yourself— it’s really testing whether you understand the null terminator: intmyStrlen(char*s){ intlen=0; while(s[len]!='\0')len++; returnlen; } voidreverseString(char*s){ intleft=0,right=myStrlen(s)-1; while(left<right){ chartemp=s[left]; s[left]=s[right]; s[right]=temp; left++; right--; 10 Data Structures & Algorithms in C

These are warm-up questions — expect them to be solved in under 5 minutes each with correct edge-case handling (empty array, single element). 2.3 Linked Lists A linked list stores elements asnodes, each holding data plus a pointer to the next node. Unlike arrays, nodes are scattered in memory — connected by pointers, not by being next to each other. List Access by indexO(1)O(n) — must walk from head Insert/delete at known positionO(n) — shiftingO(1) — if you have the pointer Insert/delete at beginningO(n)O(1) MemoryContiguous, fixed sizeScattered, grows dynamically This tradeoff — slow access but fast insertion/deletion — is the whole reason linked lists exist, and it’s exactly what interviewers probe when they ask “why would you use a linked list over an array here?” Singly linked list — full implementation: #include<stdio.h> #include<stdlib.h> structNode{ intdata; structNode*next; }; structNode*createNode(intdata){ structNode*newNode=(structNode*)malloc(sizeof(structNode)); newNode->data=data; 11 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide newNode->next=NULL; returnnewNode; } // Note the double pointer: we need to modify the caller's head variable itself voidinsertAtBeginning(structNode**head,intdata){ structNode*newNode=createNode(data); newNode->next=*head; *head=newNode; } voidinsertAtEnd(structNode**head,intdata){ structNode*newNode=createNode(data); if(*head==NULL){ *head=newNode; return; } structNode*temp=*head; while(temp->next!=NULL) temp=temp->next; temp->next=newNode; } voiddeleteNode(structNode**head,intkey){ structNode*temp=*head; structNode*prev=NULL; if(temp!=NULL&&temp->data==key){// deleting the head itself *head=temp->next; free(temp); return; } while(temp!=NULL&&temp->data!=key){ prev=temp; temp=temp->next; } if(temp==NULL)return;// key not found prev->next=temp->next; free(temp); } voidprintList(structNode*head){ structNode*temp=head; while(temp!=NULL){ printf("%d-> ",temp->data); temp=temp->next; } printf("NULL\n"); 12 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide } intmain(){ structNode*head=NULL; insertAtEnd(&head,10); insertAtEnd(&head,20); insertAtEnd(&head,30); insertAtBeginning(&head,5); printList(head);// 5 -> 10 -> 20 -> 30 ->

",temp->data); temp=temp->next; } printf("NULL\n"); 12 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide } intmain(){ structNode*head=NULL; insertAtEnd(&head,10); insertAtEnd(&head,20); insertAtEnd(&head,30); insertAtBeginning(&head,5); printList(head);// 5 -> 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> NULL deleteNode(&head,20); printList(head);// 5 -> 10 -> 30 -> NULL return0; } Reversing a linked list(the single most-asked linked list question — you should be able to write this cold): structNode*reverseList(structNode*head){ structNode*prev=NULL; structNode*curr=head; while(curr!=NULL){ structNode*nextTemp=curr->next;// save next before overwriting curr->next=prev;// reverse the pointer prev=curr;// move prev forward curr=nextTemp;// move curr forward } returnprev;// prev is the new head } Time: O(n), Space: O(1) — this iterative version beats a recursive one on space, which is worth saying out loud in an interview. Finding the middle node in one pass(fast/slow pointers — see Part 10): structNode*findMiddle(structNode*head){ structNode*slow=head,*fast=head; while(fast!=NULL&&fast->next!=NULL){ slow=slow->next; fast=fast->next->next;// fast moves 2 steps for every 1 of slow } returnslow;// when fast hits the end, slow is at the middle } Detecting a cycle(Floyd’s algorithm — also fast/slow pointers): inthasCycle(structNode*head){ structNode*slow=head,*fast=head; while(fast!=NULL&&fast->next!=NULL){ slow=slow->next; fast=fast->next->next; if(slow==fast)return1;// they meet -> there's a cycle } 13 Data Structures & Algorithms in C —

} Detecting a cycle(Floyd’s algorithm — also fast/slow pointers): inthasCycle(structNode*head){ structNode*slow=head,*fast=head; while(fast!=NULL&&fast->next!=NULL){ slow=slow->next; fast=fast->next->next; if(slow==fast)return1;// they meet -> there's a cycle } 13 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide return0; } The intuition: if fast is lapping slow on a loop, they must eventually land on the same node — like two runners on a circular track where one is twice as fast. Doubly linked list— each node also points backward, trading a bit of memory for O(1) backward traversal and easier deletion (no need to trackprevseparately while walking): structDNode{ intdata; structDNode*next; structDNode*prev; }; structDNode*createDNode(intdata){ structDNode*newNode=(structDNode*)malloc(sizeof(structDNode)); newNode->data=data; newNode->next=NULL; newNode->prev=NULL; returnnewNode; } voidinsertAtEndD(structDNode**head,intdata){ structDNode*newNode=createDNode(data); if(*head==NULL){ *head=newNode; return; } structDNode*temp=*head; while(temp->next!=NULL) temp=temp->next; temp->next=newNode; newNode->prev=temp; } voiddeleteDNode(structDNode**head,structDNode*delNode){ if(*head==delNode)*head=delNode->next; if(delNode->next!=NULL)delNode->next->prev=delNode->prev; if(delNode->prev!=NULL)delNode->prev->next=delNode->next; free(delNode); } Circular linked list— the last node points back to the head instead of NULL. Used when you need to cycle through elements repeatedly (e.g., round-robin CPU scheduling, a playlist on loop). The last plate you put on is the first one you take off. Classic application — checking balanced parentheses, a very common warm-up question: intisBalanced(char*expr){ structStack s; initStack(&s); for(inti=0;expr[i]!='\0';i++){ charch=expr[i]; if(ch=='('||ch=='{'||ch=='['){ push(&s,ch); }elseif(ch==')'||ch=='}'||ch==']'){ if(isEmpty(&s))return0; inttop=pop(&s); if((ch==')'&&top!='(')|| (ch=='}'&&top!='{')|| (ch==']'&&top!='[')) return0; } } returnisEmpty(&s);// if anything's left unmatched, it's not balanced } Other classic stack uses: evaluating postfix expressions, converting infix to postfix, undo functionality, the call stack itself (recursion IS a stack), and “next greater element” style problems. 2.5 Queues — First In, First Out (FIFO) Think of a line at a shop counter: whoever joined first gets served first. 3.4 AVL Trees — self-balancing BSTs An AVL tree is a BST that automatically keeps itself balanced, guaranteeing O(log n) for every operation — no worst-case O(n) degradation. It does this by tracking abalance factorat every node:height(left subtree) - height(right subtree). If that ever becomes -2 or +2 after an insertion, the tree performs a rotation to fix itself. 22 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide There are 4 imbalance cases, each fixed by a specific rotation: LL Case (left-left heavy)RR Case (right-right heavy) zz /\ yright-rotate(z)yleft-rotate(z) /------------->\-------------> xyxy / \/ \ x zz x LR Case: left-rotate on y, then right-rotate on z (turns it into an LL case) RL Case: right-rotate on y, then left-rotate on z (turns it into an RR case) structAVLNode{ intkey; structAVLNode*left,*right; intheight; }; intheight(structAVLNode*n){ return(n==NULL)?0:n->height; } intmaxInt(inta,intb){ return(a>b)?a:b; } intgetBalance(structAVLNode*n){ return(n==NULL)?0:height(n->left)-height(n->right); } structAVLNode*rightRotate(structAVLNode*y){ structAVLNode*x=y->left; structAVLNode*T2=x->right; x->right=y;// perform rotation y->left=T2; y->height=maxInt(height(y->left),height(y->right))+1;// update heights, y first x->height=maxInt(height(x->left),height(x->right))+1; returnx;// x is the new subtree root } structAVLNode*leftRotate(structAVLNode*x){ structAVLNode*y=x->right; structAVLNode*T2=y->left; y->left=x; 23 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide x->right=T2; x->height=maxInt(height(x->left),height(x->right))+1; y->height=maxInt(height(y->left),height(y->right))+1;

rule, stored efficiently as a plain array (no pointers needed): •Min-heap:every parent is ≤ its children (so the smallest element is always the root) •Max-heap:every parent is ≥ its children (so the largest element is always the root) Because a heap is always acompletebinary tree (filled left to right, no gaps), it can live in an array with simple index math instead of needing pointers: For a node at index i (0-indexed): parent(i) = (i - 1) / 2 left(i) = 2*i + 1 right(i) = 2*i + 2 voidswap(int*a,int*b){ inttemp=*a; *a=*b; *b=temp; } // "Sift down" — fixes a single violation by pushing the wrong element down voidminHeapify(intarr[],intn,inti){ intsmallest=i; intleft=2*i+1; intright=2*i+2; if(left<n&&arr[left]<arr[smallest]) smallest=left; if(right<n&&arr[right]<arr[smallest]) smallest=right; if(smallest!=i){ swap(&arr[i],&arr[smallest]); minHeapify(arr,n,smallest);// the swap might have broken things further down } } // Build a heap from an unsorted array — starts from the last non-leaf node voidbuildMinHeap(intarr[],intn){ for(inti=n/2-1;i>=0;i--) minHeapify(arr,n,i); } voidinsertHeap(intarr[],int*n,intvalue){ (*n)++; inti=*n-1; arr[i]=value; // "Sift up" — bubble the new value up until the heap property holds while(i!=0&&arr[(i-1)/2]>arr[i]){ 25 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide swap(&arr[i],&arr[(i-1)/2]); i=(i-1)/2; }

Heap Sort— using the heap property to sort an array in place: voidmaxHeapify(intarr[],intn,inti){ intlargest=i; intleft=2*i+1; intright=2*i+2; if(left<n&&arr[left]>arr[largest])largest=left; if(right<n&&arr[right]>arr[largest])largest=right; if(largest!=i){ swap(&arr[i],&arr[largest]); maxHeapify(arr,n,largest); } } voidheapSort(intarr[],intn){ for(inti=n/2-1;i>=0;i--)// build max heap maxHeapify(arr,n,i); for(inti=n-1;i>0;i--){ swap(&arr[0],&arr[i]);// move current max to the end maxHeapify(arr,i,0);// re-heapify the reduced heap } } 26 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide Time: O(n log n) always (no worst-case surprises like quicksort). Quick Sort— pick a pivot, partition the array so smaller elements are left of it and larger are right, then recursively sort each side. intpartition(intarr[],intlow,inthigh){ intpivot=arr[high];// choosing the last element as pivot (simple, common choice) inti=low-1; for(intj=low;j<high;j++){ if(arr[j]<pivot){ i++; swap(&arr[i],&arr[j]); 33 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide } } swap(&arr[i+1],&arr[high]); returni+1;// final position of

Here’s the mental model that makes recursive problems tractable:trust the recursive call to correctly solve the smaller subproblem, and focus only on how to combine that result into the answer for your current call.Don’t try to trace the entire call tree in your head for every problem — that’s what got you the base case + recursive case reasoning in the first place. Why naive recursive Fibonacci is a trap: intfib(intn){ if(n<=1)returnn; returnfib(n-1)+fib(n-2); } This looks innocent but is O(2ⁿ) — exponential — because it recomputes the same subproblems over and over: fib(5) /\ fib(4)fib(3) /\/\ fib(3) fib(2)fib(2) fib(1) / \/ \/ \ fib(2) fib(1) fib(1)fib(0) fib(1)fib(0) / \ fib(1)fib(0) Noticefib(3)gets computed twice,fib(2)three times. This wasted, repeated work is exactly the problem Dynamic Programming solves (Part 7) — by remembering results instead of recomputing them, the same problem drops from O(2ⁿ) to O(n). 6.2 Backtracking Backtracking is recursion

is exactly the problem Dynamic Programming solves (Part 7) — by remembering results instead of recomputing them, the same problem drops from O(2ⁿ) to O(n). 6.2 Backtracking Backtracking is recursion with a twist: try a choice, recurse, and if it doesn’t work out,undo the choice (“backtrack”) and try the next option. It’s how you systematically explore all possibilities without brute- forcing every combination blindly — you prune branches the moment you know they can’t work. The general template: voidbacktrack(/* state */){ if(/* base case: found a solution */){ // record or print it return; } for(/* each choice available */){ // make the choice if(/* choice is valid */){ backtrack(/* updated state */); } 38 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide // undo the choice (this is the "backtrack" step) } } N-Queens— place N queens on an N×N board so none attack each other (no shared row, column, or diago- nal). The classic backtracking example: #define N8 intisSafe(intboard[N][N],introw,intcol){ for(inti=0;i<col;i++)// check this row, to the left if(board[row][i])return0; for(inti=row,j=col;i>=0&&j>=0;i--,j--)// check upper-left diagonal if(board[i][j])return0; for(inti=row,j=col;i<N&&j>=0;i++,j--)// check lower-left diagonal if(board[i][j])return0;

You’ve already seen this pattern twice: •Merge Sort:Divide the array in half → Conquer (sort each half recursively) → Combine (merge the sorted halves) •Binary Search:Divide the search space in half → Conquer (search the correct half) → Combine (trivial — just return the result, no half was discarded for nothing) •QuickSort:Divide via partitioning → Conquer (sort each side) → Combine (trivial — no merge step needed, since partitioning already places elements correctly relative to each other) How to estimate the complexity of a divide-and-conquer algorithm(the simplified Master Theorem in- tuition): if a problem of sizensplits intoasubproblems each of sizen/b, plusO(n^d)work to combine them, the total complexity

How to estimate the complexity of a divide-and-conquer algorithm(the simplified Master Theorem in- tuition): if a problem of sizensplits intoasubproblems each of sizen/b, plusO(n^d)work to combine them, the total complexity depends on comparinga(how much you branch) againstb^d(how much each level shrinks): •If splitting dominates (a > b^d): complexity is roughly O(n^(log_b a)) •If combining dominates (a < b^d): complexity is roughly O(n^d) •If balanced (a = b^d): complexity is O(n^d log n) — this is exactly merge sort’s case: 2 subproblems of size n/2, O(n) to merge → O(n log n) 40 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide You don’t need the formal proof for interviews — you do need the intuition that “split into pieces, solve each, combine” naturally produces the log n factor whenever the split is roughly even. The core idea: repeatedly pick the closest unvisited vertex, and use it to try to improve (“relax”) the distances to its neighbors. #define V6 #define INF999999 47 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide intminDistance(intdist[],intvisited[]){ intmin=INF,minIndex=-1; for(intv=0;v<V;v++){ if(!visited[v]&&dist[v]<=min){ min=dist[v]; minIndex=v; } } returnminIndex; } voiddijkstra(intgraph[V][V],intsrc){ intdist[V]; intvisited[V]; for(inti=0;i<V;i++){ dist[i]=INF; visited[i]=0; } dist[src]=0; for(intcount=0;count<V-1;count++){ intu=minDistance(dist,visited);// closest unvisited vertex visited[u]=1; for(intv=0;v<V;v++){ if(!visited[v]&&graph[u][v]&& dist[u]!=INF&&dist[u]+graph[u][v]<dist[v]){ dist[v]=dist[u]+graph[u][v];// relax the edge } } } for(inti=0;i<V;i++) printf("Vertex%d-> Distance:%d\n",i,dist[i]); } This version is O(V²) becauseminDistancescans all vertices every time. returnparent[i]; } voidunionSets(intparent[],intrank[],intx,inty){ introotX=find(parent,x); introotY=find(parent,y); if(rootX==rootY)return;// already in the same set if(rank[rootX]<rank[rootY]){// union by rank: attach smaller tree under bigger↪ parent[rootX]=rootY; }elseif(rank[rootX]>rank[rootY]){ parent[rootY]=rootX; }else{ parent[rootY]=rootX; rank[rootX]++; } } // Initialization: every element starts as its own parent (its own set) voidinitUnionFind(intparent[],intrank[],intn){ for(inti=0;i<n;i++){ parent[i]=i; rank[i]=0; } } With both path compression and union by rank, operations run in nearly O(1) amortized time (technically O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function — for any input size you’d ever encounter, this is effectively constant). 8.4 Topological Sort For aDirected Acyclic Graph (DAG), a topological sort orders vertices so that every edgeu -> vhasu appearing beforev. Kruskal’s Algorithm— sort all edges by weight, greedily add each edge unless it would create a cycle (checked via Union-Find): structEdge{ intsrc,dest,weight; }; intcompareEdges(constvoid*a,constvoid*b){ return((structEdge*)a)->weight-((structEdge*)b)->weight; } voidkruskalMST(structEdge edges[],intV,intE){ qsort(edges,E,sizeof(structEdge),compareEdges); 50 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide int*parent=(int*)malloc(V*sizeof(int)); int*rank=(int*)malloc(V*sizeof(int)); initUnionFind(parent,rank,V); intmstWeight=0,edgeCount=0; for(inti=0;i<E&&edgeCount<V-1;i++){ intx=find(parent,edges[i].src); inty=find(parent,edges[i].dest); if(x!=y){// adding this edge won't create a cycle unionSets(parent,rank,x,y); mstWeight+=edges[i].weight; edgeCount++; printf("Edge:%d-%d, Weight:%d

Prim’sAlgorithmsolves the same MST problem with a different strategy — grow a single tree one edge at a time, always adding the cheapest edge that connects a new vertex to the tree. It’s structurally very similar to Dijkstra (both repeatedly pick a “closest” vertex), except Prim’s compares edge weights directly rather than accumulated path distance. Worth knowing the distinction if asked to compare them, even without writing the full code. Spotting which pattern applies is often the actual skill being tested. 10.1 Two Pointers Use two indices moving through a structure (often from both ends, or one ahead of the other) instead of nested loops — turns many O(n²) brute-force approaches into O(n). Pair with a given sum in a sorted array: voidpairSum(intarr[],intn,inttarget){ intleft=0,right=n-1; while(left<right){ intsum=arr[left]+arr[right]; if(sum==target){ printf("Pair found:%d+%d\n",arr[left],arr[right]); return; }elseif(sum<target){ left++;// need a bigger sum -> move left pointer up }else{ right--;// need a smaller sum -> move right pointer down } } printf("No pair found\n"); } Removeduplicatesfromasortedarrayinplace— a slow pointer tracks the “write” position, a fast pointer scans ahead: intremoveDuplicates(intarr[],intn){ if(n==0)return0; intslow=0; for(intfast=1;fast<n;fast++){ if(arr[fast]!=arr[slow]){ slow++; arr[slow]=arr[fast]; } } returnslow+1;// new length } 10.2 Sliding Window For problems about contiguous subarrays/substrings, avoid recomputing the whole window from scratch each time — instead, slide it: remove the element leaving the window, add the element entering it. Maximum sum subarray of fixed size k: 54 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide intmaxSumSubarray(intarr[],intn,intk){ intwindowSum=0; for(inti=0;i<k;i++)windowSum+=arr[i];// first window intmaxSum=windowSum; for(inti=k;i<n;i++){ windowSum+=arr[i]-arr[i-k];// slide: add new element, drop the oldest if(windowSum>maxSum)maxSum=windowSum; }

fixed size k: 54 Data Structures & Algorithms in C — Interview Guide intmaxSumSubarray(intarr[],intn,intk){ intwindowSum=0; for(inti=0;i<k;i++)windowSum+=arr[i];// first window intmaxSum=windowSum; for(inti=k;i<n;i++){ windowSum+=arr[i]-arr[i-k];// slide: add new element, drop the oldest if(windowSum>maxSum)maxSum=windowSum; } returnmaxSum; } This is O(n) instead of the O(n×k) you’d get recomputing every window’s sum from scratch. Variable-size window(e.g., longest substring without repeating characters) works the same way, but the window grows/shrinks based on a condition rather than a fixed sizek— grow the right edge until the condi- tion breaks, then shrink the left edge until it’s valid again. 10.3 Fast & Slow Pointers Already seen in Part 2.3 (cycle detection, finding the middle of a linked list) — worth calling out as its own named pattern because once you recognize “cycle” or “middle element in one pass,” this is almost always the tool. 10.4 Top-K Elements (via Heap) Whenever a problem asks for the K largest/smallest/most-frequent elements, a heap (Part 3.5) is almost always the efficient answer — maintain a heap of size K as you scan through the data, giving O(n log k) instead of the O(n log

In C, you build the node, you allocate its memory yourself, and you wire the pointers yourself. That is slower at first and much better for actually understanding what a data structure is. This chapter covers only what you need for the rest of the book -- it is not a complete C course. 1.1 Your first program and how to run it #include <stdio.h> int main() { printf("Hello, DSA!\n"); return 0; } Save this as hello.c. On any Linux/Mac terminal, or on Windows with MinGW installed, compile and run it with: gcc hello.c -o hello ./hello gcc is the compiler. In C, you must state what type of value the box will hold, because C uses that information to know how many bytes to reserve and how to interpret them. int age = 15; // whole numbers, usually 4 bytes float price = 99.50f; // decimal numbers, 4 bytes, ~7 digit precision double pi = 3.14159265; // decimal numbers, 8 bytes, more precision char grade = 'A'; // a single character, 1 byte int typically ranges from about -2.1 billion to 2.1 billion. billion. When a number can exceed that (like counting something huge, or multiplying two large numbers), you will need long long, which typically gives you about -9.2 quintillion to 9.2 quintillion. This matters a lot in interviews: a very common bug is an int overflowing silently when two large numbers are multiplied. When in doubt for competitive problems, use long long. long long big = 10000000000LL; // the LL suffix tells the compiler this is long long DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 7 1.3 Operators int a = 7, b = 2; int sum = a + b; // 9 int diff = a - b; // 5 int prod = a * b; // 14 int quot = a / b; // 3 -- integer division truncates toward zero int rem = a % b; // 1 -- modulo, the remainder after division Comparison operators (<, >, <=, >=, ==, !=) produce 1 (true) or 0 (false) in C -- there is no separate boolean type in classic C (C99 added _Bool, but you will mostly

Changing x inside the function does not change the original variable in the caller. This is exactly why pointers (section 1.7) matter so much -- they are how you let a function actually modify something back in the caller. 1.6 Arrays An array is a block of memory holding many values of the same type, laid out back to back, so the computer can jump straight to any index in constant time. int nums[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { printf("%d ", nums[i]); } nums[2] = 99; // overwrite index 2 -> {10, 20, 99, 40, 50} nums[i] is actually shorthand for "go

= 0; i < 5; i++) { printf("%d ", nums[i]); } nums[2] = 99; // overwrite index 2 -> {10, 20, 99, 40, 50} nums[i] is actually shorthand for "go to the start of nums, move i slots forward, and read that value." Because each int takes a fixed number of bytes, the computer can calculate exactly where index i lives without scanning anything -- that is why array access is O(1). Two-dimensional arrays are just arrays of arrays: int grid[3][4]; // 3 rows, 4 columns for (int r = 0; r < 3; r++) { for (int c = 0; c < 4; c++) { grid[r][c] = r * 4 + c; } } C arrays do not know their own length -- unlike Python lists, a C array has no .length. You must track the size yourself in a separate variable, almost always. This is one of the most common sources of bugs for beginners: forgetting to pass the size alongside the array into a function. 1.7 Pointers -- the most important idea in this chapter A pointer

This sounds abstract, so build the mental picture directly: imagine memory as a huge street of numbered houses. A normal variable is a house with a value inside. A pointer is a house whose value is the address of another house. DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 9 int x = 10; int *p = &x; // p now holds the address of x printf("%d
", x); // 10 -- the value printf("%p

This is exactly how you make call-by-value functions modify the caller's variable: void increment(int *p) { *p = *p + 1; } int main() { int x = 5; increment(&x); printf("%d

Common string functions (from <string.h>): #include <string.h> char a[20] = "hello"; char b[20] = "world"; strlen(a); // 5 -- length NOT counting '\0' strcpy(a, b); // copies b into a -- a is now "world" strcat(a, b); // appends b onto a strcmp(a, b); // 0 if equal, <0 if a<b alphabetically, >0 if a>b For DSA problems, you will often treat a string as char arr[] and index into it like any array (arr[i]), scanning with strlen(arr) as the bound. 1.9 Structures A struct bundles several related variables under one name. build every custom data structure in this book -- nodes, tree cells, graph edges, all of them are structs. struct Point { int x; int y; }; int main() { struct Point p1; p1.x = 3; p1.y = 4; printf("(%d, %d)\n", p1.x, p1.y); return 0; } DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 11 Writing struct Point everywhere is repetitive, so typedef gives the type a shorter alias -- you will see this pattern in almost every code block in this book: typedef struct { int x; int y; } Point; Point p1 = {3, 4}; When you have a pointer to a struct, use -> instead of . to reach its fields -- -> is shorthand for "dereference, then access a field": Point *pp = &p1; printf("%d\n", pp->x); // same as (*pp).x 1.10 Dynamic memory allocation Every array or struct you have seen so far lives on the "stack" -- memory that is reserved automatically when a function starts and freed automatically when it returns. The stack cannot grow a data structure at runtime, and it disappears the

"stack" -- memory that is reserved automatically when a function starts and freed automatically when it returns. The stack cannot grow a data structure at runtime, and it disappears the moment the function ends. Data structures like linked lists, trees, and graphs need memory that survives beyond a single function call and can grow as needed -- that memory comes from the "heap," and you manage it yourself using <stdlib.h>. #include <stdlib.h> int *arr = (int *) malloc(5 * sizeof(int)); // room for 5 ints, uninitialized if (arr == NULL) { // malloc failed -- always check this in real code } for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { arr[i] = i * i; } free(arr); // give the memory back -- REQUIRED, or it is a memory leak arr = NULL; // good practice: avoid an accidental "dangling pointer" reuse • malloc(n) reserves n bytes and returns a pointer to the start, uninitialized (garbage values). sizeof(int) gives the exact byte size of an int on your machine, so malloc(5 * sizeof(int)) reads as "room for 5

returns a pointer to the start, uninitialized (garbage values). sizeof(int) gives the exact byte size of an int on your machine, so malloc(5 * sizeof(int)) reads as "room for 5 ints." • calloc(n, size) does the same but zero-initializes the memory. • realloc(ptr, newSize) grows or shrinks a previous allocation, copying old data over automatically. • free(ptr) returns memory to the system. Every malloc/calloc must eventually be matched with exactly one free. Forgetting this is a memory leak; calling free twice on the same pointer, or using memory after freeing it, is undefined behavior and a classic interview red flag if you do it live. This is the single mechanism that makes linked lists, trees, and graphs possible in C: every "node" you create in this entire book is a malloc'd struct, wired to other malloc'd structs by pointers. DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 12 1.11 Recursion, in brief (full chapter later) A recursive function is one that calls itself on a smaller version of the same problem, until it hits a case simple enough to answer

in brief (full chapter later) A recursive function is one that calls itself on a smaller version of the same problem, until it hits a case simple enough to answer directly (the "base case"). int factorial(int n) { if (n <= 1) return 1; // base case return n * factorial(n - 1); // recursive case: smaller problem } Every recursive call gets its own space on the call stack, holding its own copy of n. factorial(4) waits on 4 * factorial(3), which waits on 3 * factorial(2), and so on, until factorial(1) returns 1 directly and the multiplications unwind back up. Chapter 5 goes deep on this; for now, just recognize the shape: base case checked first, recursive case calls the same function on a smaller input. 1.12 Common beginner mistakes (that also show up in interviews) • Segmentation fault ("segfault"): reading or writing memory you do not own -- most often, dereferencing a NULL pointer, or an uninitialized pointer, or reading past the end of an array. • Off-by-one errors: looping i <= n when it should be

-- most often, dereferencing a NULL pointer, or an uninitialized pointer, or reading past the end of an array. • Off-by-one errors: looping i <= n when it should be i < n, or the reverse. Always double check your loop bound against a size-1 array by hand. • Memory leaks: malloc without a matching free. Not fatal in a 5-minute LeetCode run, but interviewers notice if you never free anything you allocate. • Comparing strings with ==: str1 == str2 compares addresses, not contents. Always use strcmp(str1, str2) == 0 for content comparison. • Integer overflow: adding two large ints that exceed about 2.1 billion wraps around silently instead of erroring. Use long long when values could get large. • Uninitialized variables: an int x; with no assignment holds garbage, not 0. Always initialize before reading. With this foundation, you have everything you need to build every data structure in this book. Complexity Analysis -- Big-O and Friends 2.1 Why this chapter matters more than it looks like it does Every LeetCode problem has hidden constraints, like "1 <= n <= 100000." Those numbers are a direct hint about which complexity is required. If n can be 100,000, an O(n^2) solution will run roughly 10,000,000,000 basic operations -- far too slow, and your submission will fail with "Time Limit Exceeded" even if the logic is perfectly correct. Learning to read those constraints and match them to a complexity class is one of the highest-leverage skills in this entire book. 2.2 What Big-O actually measures Big-O describes how the runtime (or memory) of an algorithm grows as the input size n grows, ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms. A recursive function that makes 2 calls on n-1 each time (like naive Fibonacci) roughly doubles its call count each level down, giving O(2^n). Chapter 5 and Chapter 15 return to this with recursion trees, which make it visual. 2.6 Big-O vs Big-Omega vs Big-Theta These three describe upper, lower, and tight bounds on growth: • Big-O (O): an upper bound -- "this algorithm never does more than roughly this much work." This is what people mean colloquially when they say "the complexity is O(n)." • Big-Omega (Omega): a lower bound -- "this algorithm always does

more than roughly this much work." This is what people mean colloquially when they say "the complexity is O(n)." • Big-Omega (Omega): a lower bound -- "this algorithm always does at least roughly this much work." DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 15 • Big-Theta (Theta): a tight bound -- both the upper and lower bound match, meaning the algorithm's growth rate is exactly this, not just bounded by it. In interviews, people almost always say "O(...)" even when they technically mean Theta. You will not lose points for doing the same -- just know the distinction exists if it comes up. 2.7 Space complexity Space complexity measures extra memory used, beyond the input itself, as a function of n. A function that uses a fixed number of extra variables regardless of input size is O(1) extra space ("in-place"). A function that builds a new array of size n is O(n) extra space. A recursive function also spends O(depth) space on the call stack, even if it allocates no extra arrays -- this is easy to forget and

n is O(n) extra space. The classic example: a dynamic array (like a resizable array) that doubles its capacity when full. Any single append that triggers a resize costs O(n) that one time, but because doublings become exponentially rarer, the average cost per append across many calls is O(1). You will see "amortized O(1)" used for exactly this kind of operation. With a working vocabulary for "how fast," you are ready to start on real data structures. Recursion and Backtracking 5.1 Recursion, properly this time A recursive function solves a problem by solving a smaller instance of the same problem, and combining that result. Every recursive function needs exactly two parts: • Base case: the smallest input, answered directly, with no further recursion. Without this, the function calls itself forever (a "stack overflow" crash). • Recursive case: expresses the problem in terms of a smaller version of itself. 5.2 Seeing the call stack: the Fibonacci recursion tree int fib(int n) { if (n <= 1) return n; // base case return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2); // recursive case } Draw out fib(4) as a tree of calls, and something important becomes visible: fib(4)

n; // base case return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2); // recursive case } Draw out fib(4) as a tree of calls, and something important becomes visible: fib(4) / \ fib(3) fib(2) / \ / \ fib(2) fib(1) fib(1) fib(0) / \ fib(1) fib(0) fib(2) is computed twice, fib(1) is computed three times. This wasted, repeated work is exactly why naive recursive Fibonacci is O(2^n) -- and exactly the problem that Dynamic Programming (Chapter 15) exists to fix, by remembering answers instead of recomputing them. Keep this tree in mind; it is the single clearest picture of "why DP" you will encounter in this book. 5.3 Tail recursion, briefly A recursive call is "tail recursive" if it is the very last thing the function does, with no pending work after it returns (like the multiplication in factorial, which is not tail recursive, since n * still has to happen after the recursive call returns). Some languages optimize tail calls into a loop internally to avoid growing the call stack; standard C compilers generally do not guarantee this optimization,

Some languages optimize tail calls into a loop internally to avoid growing the call stack; standard C compilers generally do not guarantee this optimization, so very deep tail-recursive C code can still overflow the stack. For DSA interview purposes, know the term, but do not rely on tail-call optimization in C. 5.4 Backtracking: recursion with undo Backtracking solves problems by exploring choices one at a time, going deeper with each choice, and undoing a choice to try the next alternative when a path does not lead anywhere useful (or once you have recorded it as a valid answer). The pattern is always the same shape: DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 26 explore(state): if state is a complete valid answer: record it return for each choice available from state: make the choice (change state) explore(new state) (recurse) undo the choice (restore state -- THIS is "backtracking") The "undo" step is what makes it backtracking rather than plain recursion: after trying a branch, you restore the exact state you had before, so the

Trying both choices for every element, recursively, generates every possible subset. int arr[] = {1, 2, 3}; int n = 3; int current[10], currentSize = 0; void printSubset() { printf("{ "); for (int i = 0; i < currentSize; i++) printf("%d ", current[i]); printf("}\n"); } void subsets(int index) { if (index == n) { printSubset(); return; } subsets(index + 1); // choice 1: exclude arr[index] current[currentSize++] = arr[index]; // choice 2: include arr[index] subsets(index + 1); currentSize--; // undo -- backtrack } For n = 3 this produces all 2^3 = 8 subsets, because each of the 3 elements doubles the number of branches. 5.6 Permutations Every position in the output can be filled by any element not yet used. A monotonic stack (a stack kept always increasing or always decreasing) solves it in O(n). void nextGreaterElement(int arr[], int n, int result[]) { int stack[100]; int top = -1; for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) { while (top != -1 && stack[top] <= arr[i]) { top--; // pop values that can never be the answer for anyone to their left } result[i] = (top == -1) ? -1 : stack[top]; stack[++top] = arr[i]; } } Scanning right to left, the stack always holds a decreasing sequence of "candidates." Anything smaller than or equal to the current element can never be the next-greater answer for anything further left (the current element is a better, closer candidate), so it is permanently discarded. Each element is pushed once and popped at most once, which is what makes the whole scan O(n) despite the inner while loop. 7.4 Queue -- First In, First Out (FIFO) A queue only adds at the "rear" and removes from the "front," like a line of people -- first one in line is

Queue -- First In, First Out (FIFO) A queue only adds at the "rear" and removes from the "front," like a line of people -- first one in line is the first one served. Circular array implementation (avoids wasting slots as a plain array queue would): DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 37 #define QMAX 100 typedef struct { int data[QMAX]; int front, rear, size; } Queue; void initQueue(Queue* q) { q->front = 0; q->rear = -1; q->size = 0; } void enqueue(Queue* q, int val) { if (q->size == QMAX) return; // full q->rear = (q->rear + 1) % QMAX; q->data[q->rear] = val; q->size++; } int dequeue(Queue* q) { if (q->size == 0) return -1; // empty int val = q->data[q->front]; q->front = (q->front + 1) % QMAX; q->size--; return val; } The % QMAX is what makes it "circular" -- once rear reaches the last slot, the next enqueue wraps back around to index 0, reusing space that earlier dequeues freed up, instead of needing to shift everything or running out of room permanently. A binary tree restricts every node to at most two children, conventionally called left and right. The height of a tree is the number of nodes on the longest path from root to a leaf (so an empty tree has height 0, a single node has height 1) -- this is the

the number of nodes on the longest path from root to a leaf (so an empty tree has height 0, a single node has height 1) -- this is the same convention LeetCode uses for "maximum depth" problems. Preorder is the natural order for copying a tree (you visit a node before its children, so you always create a parent DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 40 before its kids). Postorder is the natural order for deleting a tree or evaluating an expression tree (children fully processed before the parent needs their results). 8.3 Level order traversal (breadth-first) Depth-first traversals use the call stack implicitly. their results at the current node. 8.5 Binary Search Trees (BST) The BST property: for every node, everything in its left subtree is smaller, and everything in its right subtree is bigger. This one rule is what makes search, insert, and delete all O(height) instead of O(n) -- and it is exactly why an inorder traversal of a BST visits every value in sorted order: you always fully exhaust "everything smaller" (the left subtree) before visiting the node itself, then move on to "everything bigger" (the right subtree). If you insert already-sorted data into a plain BST, it degenerates into a straight line -- height O(n), and every operation becomes as slow as a linked list. Self-balancing trees (AVL trees, red-black trees) fix this by rotating nodes after insertions and deletions to keep the height at O(log n) no matter the insertion order. Heaps and Priority Queues 9.1 What a heap is for A heap answers one question extremely fast, over and over as data changes: "what is the minimum (or maximum) element right now?" A min-heap keeps the smallest element always at the root, in O(1) to peek, O(log n) to insert or remove it. This is exactly what a priority queue needs -- process items in order of priority, where priority can change as new items arrive. 9.2 The array trick behind every heap A heap is a complete binary tree (every level full except possibly the last, filled left to right) -- and a complete binary tree can be stored in a plain array, with no pointers at all, using pure arithmetic to find parent/child relationships: for index i: parent index = (i - 1) / 2 left child = 2*i

a plain array, with no pointers at all, using pure arithmetic to find parent/child relationships: for index i: parent index = (i - 1) / 2 left child = 2*i + 1 right child = 2*i + 2 9.3 Min-heap: insert (sift up) and extract-min (sift down) DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 44 #define MAXHEAP 1000 typedef struct { int data[MAXHEAP]; int size; } MinHeap; void swap(int* a, int* b) { int temp = *a; *a = *b; *b = temp; } void siftUp(MinHeap* h, int i) { while (i > 0) { int parent = (i - 1) / 2; if (h->data[parent] <= h->data[i]) break; swap(&h->data[parent], &h->data[i]); i = parent; } } void insert(MinHeap* h, int val) { h->data[h->size] = val; siftUp(h, h->size); h->size++; } void siftDown(MinHeap* h, int i) { while (1) { int left = 2 * i + 1, right = 2 * i + 2; int smallest = i; if (left < h->size && h->data[left] < h->data[smallest]) smallest = left; if (right < h->size && h->data[right] < h->data[smallest]) smallest = right; if

i + 2; int smallest = i; if (left < h->size && h->data[left] < h->data[smallest]) smallest = left; if (right < h->size && h->data[right] < h->data[smallest]) smallest = right; if (smallest == i) break; swap(&h->data[i], &h->data[smallest]); i = smallest; } } int extractMin(MinHeap* h) { int minVal = h->data[0]; h->size--; h->data[0] = h->data[h->size]; // move the last element to the root siftDown(h, 0); // then restore the heap property return minVal; } siftUp fixes a violation by bubbling a value toward the root; siftDown fixes a violation by bubbling a value toward the leaves. Both only ever travel along one path from root to leaf (or leaf to root), which is why both are O(log n) -- the height of a complete binary tree with n nodes is always O(log n). 9.4 Building a heap from an array in O(n), not O(n log n) You could insert n elements one at a time (O(n log n) total), but there is a faster way: place all elements in the array as-is, then sift down starting from the last non-leaf node backward

at a time (O(n log n) total), but there is a faster way: place all elements in the array as-is, then sift down starting from the last non-leaf node backward to the root. DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 45 void buildHeap(MinHeap* h, int arr[], int n) { for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) h->data[i] = arr[i]; h->size = n; for (int i = n / 2 - 1; i >= 0; i--) { siftDown(h, i); } } This is O(n) overall, not O(n log n), because most nodes in a heap sit near the bottom and only need to sift down a short distance -- only the few nodes near the root have far to travel, and the math works out to a linear total rather than n times log n. (You do not need to reprove this in an interview -- just know "heapify an array" is O(n), which often surprises people.) 9.5 Heap sort Build a max-heap, then repeatedly swap the maximum (the root) to the end of the array and shrink

"heapify an array" is O(n), which often surprises people.) 9.5 Heap sort Build a max-heap, then repeatedly swap the maximum (the root) to the end of the array and shrink the heap by one, restoring the heap property each time. The result is a fully in-place, O(n log n) worst-case sort using O(1) extra space. void siftDownMax(int arr[], int n, int i) { while (1) { int left = 2 * i + 1, right = 2 * i + 2; int largest = i; if (left < n && arr[left] > arr[largest]) largest = left; if (right < n && arr[right] > arr[largest]) largest = right; if (largest == i) break; int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[largest]; arr[largest] = temp; i = largest; } } void heapSort(int arr[], int n) { for (int i = n / 2 - 1; i >= 0; i--) { siftDownMax(arr, n, i); // build max-heap } for (int i = n - 1; i > 0; i--) { int temp = arr[0]; arr[0] = arr[i]; arr[i] = temp; // move max to

// build max-heap } for (int i = n - 1; i > 0; i--) { int temp = arr[0]; arr[0] = arr[i]; arr[i] = temp; // move max to the end siftDownMax(arr, i, 0); // heap "shrinks" to size i } } 9.6 The "top-K elements" pattern Problem shape: find the K largest (or smallest, or most frequent) elements among n items. Keep a min-heap of size K as you scan: push each new element, and whenever the heap exceeds size K, pop the minimum. Whatever survives in the heap at the end is exactly the K largest elements seen -- and every push/pop only costs O(log K), giving O(n log K) total, which beats sorting everything (O(n log n)) whenever K is small compared to n. Advanced Graph Algorithms 13.1 Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) What it answers, fast: "are these two elements in the same group?" and "merge these two groups into one," both in almost O(1) time (technically O(alpha(n)), an essentially-constant function called the inverse Ackermann function). This is the engine behind Kruskal's algorithm below, and behind many "group the connected things" problems on its own. int parent[MAXV], rank_[MAXV]; void makeSet(int V) { for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) { parent[i] = i; rank_[i] = 0; } } int find(int x) { if (parent[x] != x) { parent[x] = find(parent[x]); //

(int i = 0; i < V; i++) { parent[i] = i; rank_[i] = 0; } } int find(int x) { if (parent[x] != x) { parent[x] = find(parent[x]); // path compression: flatten the tree } return parent[x]; } void unionSets(int x, int y) { int rootX = find(x), rootY = find(y); if (rootX == rootY) return; if (rank_[rootX] < rank_[rootY]) { parent[rootX] = rootY; } else if (rank_[rootX] > rank_[rootY]) { parent[rootY] = rootX; } else { parent[rootY] = rootX; rank_[rootX]++; } } Two optimizations make this almost-constant-time: path compression (every node find visits gets pointed straight at the root, so future lookups are instant) and union by rank (always attach the shorter tree under the taller one, so trees stay flat). Greedy rule: sort by finish time, and always take the next activity whose start is not before the last one you accepted. typedef struct Activity { int start, finish; } Activity; int compareActivities(const void* a, const void* b) { return ((Activity*)a)->finish - ((Activity*)b)->finish; } int maxActivities(Activity acts[], int n) { qsort(acts, n, sizeof(Activity), compareActivities); int count = 1; int lastFinish = acts[0].finish; for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) { if (acts[i].start >= lastFinish) { count++; lastFinish = acts[i].finish; } } return count; } Why finishing earliest is provably the

Any optimal solution that picked a different first activity can always be rewritten to start with the earliest-finishing one instead, without making the total count any worse -- this kind of argument is called an "exchange argument," and it is the standard way greedy correctness gets justified. 14.3 Fractional knapsack Problem: fill a bag of limited weight capacity with items (each with a weight and a value) to maximize total value, where items can be split into fractions. Greedy rule: always take as much as possible of the item with the best value-to-weight ratio first. Greedy rule: repeatedly take the two least-frequent remaining nodes and merge them into a new node (with combined frequency), until one tree remains -- notice this is exactly the min-heap "always grab the smallest" pattern from Chapter 9, applied greedily. The path from the root to each character's leaf becomes its code; more frequent characters end up closer to the root and get shorter codes. 14.5 Practice problems • Jump Game • Jump Game II • Gas Station • Non-overlapping Intervals DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 63

Dynamic Programming 15.1 Why DP exists -- go back to the recursion tree Look back at the Fibonacci recursion tree in Section 5.2. fib(2) was computed three separate times. Dynamic Programming is simply the idea of remembering the answer to a subproblem the first time you compute it, so every subsequent request for that same subproblem is a free lookup instead of repeated work. It applies exactly when a problem has: • Overlapping subproblems: the same smaller subproblem gets solved repeatedly (as seen in the Fibonacci tree). • Optimal substructure: the optimal answer to the whole problem can be built from optimal answers to its subproblems. 15.2 Two ways to remember: memoization vs tabulation Memoization (top-down): keep the natural recursive structure, but check a cache before

problem can be built from optimal answers to its subproblems. 15.2 Two ways to remember: memoization vs tabulation Memoization (top-down): keep the natural recursive structure, but check a cache before recomputing, and fill the cache after computing. int memo[100]; bool computed[100] = {false}; int fibMemo(int n) { if (n <= 1) return n; if (computed[n]) return memo[n]; memo[n] = fibMemo(n - 1) + fibMemo(n - 2); computed[n] = true; return memo[n]; } Tabulation (bottom-up): flip the direction entirely -- start from the base cases and iteratively build up to the answer, with no recursion at all. int fibTab(int n) { if (n <= 1) return n; int dp[n + 1]; dp[0] = 0; dp[1] = 1; for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) { dp[i] = dp[i - 1] + dp[i - 2]; } return dp[n]; } Space-optimized: once you notice dp[i] only ever depends on the previous two values, there is no need to keep the whole table at all. DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 65 int fibOptimal(int n) { if (n <= 1) return n; int prev2 = 0, prev1 = 1; for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) { int curr = prev1 + prev2; prev2 = prev1; prev1 = curr; } return prev1; } Four versions of the exact same problem: naive recursion is O(2^n) time; memoization and tabulation are both O(n) time, O(n) space; the space-optimized version is O(n) time, O(1) space. This progression -- notice the repeated work, cache it, then notice you can shrink the cache -- is the mental checklist to run on every new DP problem. 15.3 The same shape in disguise: Climbing Stairs Problem: you can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time; how many distinct ways to reach step n? The number of ways to reach step n is the ways to reach step n-1 (then take one final step) plus the ways to reach step n-2 (then take a final

number of ways to reach step n is the ways to reach step n-1 (then take one final step) plus the ways to reach step n-2 (then take a final double step) -- which is exactly the Fibonacci recurrence, wearing a different costume. int climbStairs(int n) { if (n <= 2) return n; int prev2 = 1, prev1 = 2; for (int i = 3; i <= n; i++) { int curr = prev1 + prev2; prev2 = prev1; prev1 = curr; } return prev1; } This is the single most valuable habit Dynamic Programming rewards: learning to spot that a brand-new-looking problem has the same recurrence shape as one you have already solved. 15.4 0/1 Knapsack -- the first 2D DP Problem: given item weights and values, and a capacity, choose a subset (each item taken whole or not at all) that maximizes total value without exceeding capacity. Subtracting 1 flips that single bit to 0 and every bit below it to 1 (7 = 0111). ANDing the two only produces 0 when there was exactly one bit set to begin with. 16.3 Count set bits -- Brian Kernighan's algorithm int countSetBits(int n) { int count = 0; while (n != 0) { n = n & (n - 1); // clears the lowest set bit count++; } return count; } n & (n - 1) always clears exactly the lowest set bit (the same trick as above, just used repeatedly). This loop runs once per set bit, not once per bit position -- so for a number with only a few set bits, it is much faster than checking all 32 positions one at a time. 16.4 Find the single unique number (XOR trick) Problem: every number in an array appears exactly twice, except one. Find it, in O(n) time and O(1) space. DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 71 int findUnique(int arr[], int n) { int result = 0; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { result = result ^ arr[i]; } return result; } XOR-ing any number with itself gives 0, and XOR is commutative and associative, so every pair in the array cancels itself out no matter what order they appear in, leaving only the number with no partner. 16.5 Get, set, clear, and toggle a bit int getBit(int n, int i) { return (n >> i) & 1; } int setBit(int n, int i) { return n | (1 << i); } int

toggle a bit int getBit(int n, int i) { return (n >> i) & 1; } int setBit(int n, int i) { return n | (1 << i); } int clearBit(int n, int i) { return n & ~(1 << i); } int toggleBit(int n, int i) { return n ^ (1 << i); } 1 << i builds a "mask" with only bit i turned on; combining that mask with &, |, or ^ against the original number reads, sets, or flips exactly that one bit without disturbing any other. 16.6 Practice problems • Single Number • Number of 1 Bits • Counting Bits • Reverse Bits • Missing Number • Sum of Two Integers (without using + or -) • Power of Two DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 72 17. A plain array makes update O(1) but range sum O(n); a prefix sum array makes range sum O(1) but update O(n). A segment tree makes both O(log n), by storing a binary tree over the array where every node summarizes the range it covers. #define MAXN 100000 int tree[4 * MAXN]; int arr[MAXN]; void build(int node, int start, int end) { if (start == end) { tree[node] = arr[start]; return; } int mid = (start + end) / 2; build(2 * node, start, mid); build(2 * node + 1, mid + 1, end); tree[node] = tree[2 * node] + tree[2 * node + 1]; } void update(int node, int start, int end, int idx, int val)

Placement Preparation Roadmap 19.1 How to structure practice over time Practice in three distinct phases, in this order -- doing them out of order is the most common reason people plateau: 1. Topic-wise (most of your prep time): work one chapter at a time, right after reading it, so the pattern is fresh. Do not mix topics yet -- the goal here is depth on one idea at a time. 2. Mixed, timed practice: once most topics are covered, switch to random problems with a strict timer (45 minutes each), with no idea in advance which pattern applies. This is what actually trains pattern recognition under pressure, which topic-wise practice cannot. 3. Mock interviews: practice explaining your thinking out loud to another

idea in advance which pattern applies. This is what actually trains pattern recognition under pressure, which topic-wise practice cannot. 3. Mock interviews: practice explaining your thinking out loud to another person (a friend, a mentor, or a mock-interview platform), not just solving silently. Solving alone and solving while narrating your thought process are different skills, and only the second one is what gets evaluated in a real interview. 19.2 A 10-week roadmap through this book WeekFocusRough problem count 1 C fundamentals, complexity analysis, array/string patterns (Ch. 1-3) 15 2 Searching, sorting, recursion, backtracking (Ch. 4-5) 15 3 Linked lists, stacks, queues (Ch. 6-7) 15 4Trees, BSTs, heaps (Ch. 8-9)15 5 Hashing, tries, graph basics (Ch. 10-12) 15 6 Advanced graph algorithms, greedy (Ch. 13-14) 12 7 Dynamic programming, in real depth (Ch. 15) 20 8 Bit manipulation, bonus structures, start the pattern playbook (Ch. 16-18) 15 9 Mixed, timed, random-order practice across all topics 20-25 10 Mock interviews, resume polish, rest before real interviews -- DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 79 Adjust the pace to your own

"Built a REST API" says less than "Built a REST API handling 10,000 requests/day, cutting response time by 40%." • 2-4 real projects, described in one line each: what it does, what you built it with, and a concrete result or capability. Depth on a few projects beats a long, shallow list. • Keep it to one page for a student or early-career resume. Anything a reviewer has to hunt for gets skipped. • List your competitive programming / practice profile (LeetCode, Codeforces, GitHub) if it reflects real, sustained effort -- it gives a recruiter a concrete, checkable signal beyond the resume text itself. • Tailor to the role: a pure software engineering role

if it reflects real, sustained effort -- it gives a recruiter a concrete, checkable signal beyond the resume text itself. • Tailor to the role: a pure software engineering role wants to see DSA practice, systems/project work, and CS fundamentals front and center; a research-adjacent role wants publications or research projects placed above generic coursework. 19.4 How to actually behave in a mock or real interview • Think out loud, constantly, even (especially) when stuck. An interviewer cannot evaluate silence, and cannot give you a nudge in the right direction if they do not know where your thinking currently is. • Clarify before coding: input size, duplicates, sorted or not, what to do on invalid input. This is not stalling -- it is exactly what Section 18.1 recommends, and it consistently reads as strong signal rather than weakness. Ask about the input size specifically -- it tells you the expected complexity (Chapter 2). • State the brute force out loud first, even when you already see a better approach. Naming the brute force, and then naming specifically what is inefficient

complexity (Chapter 2). • State the brute force out loud first, even when you already see a better approach. Naming the brute force, and then naming specifically what is inefficient about it, is what actually demonstrates you understand why the optimized approach works, rather than having only memorized it. • Trace your code on a real example by hand once it is written -- do not just glance at it and declare it correct. • State time and space complexity unprompted at the end. Interviewers consistently rate this well, and it is a final chance to catch your own mistake. 19.5 Common mistakes that cost more than they should • Jumping straight into code with no stated plan. • Staying silent while thinking, instead of narrating. • Having memorized a specific solution instead of the underlying pattern -- this breaks the moment the problem is even slightly modified from the version you practiced. • Ignoring the hints hidden in the stated constraints (Chapter 2's table exists specifically to train you out of this). • Declaring the solution finished without testing

version you practiced. • Ignoring the hints hidden in the stated constraints (Chapter 2's table exists specifically to train you out of this). • Declaring the solution finished without testing it against even one example. Do not cram a brand-new topic the night before -- at this point, review beats new material every time. • Skim Chapter 18's pattern table once, as a warm-up, not as new learning. • For virtual interviews: test your coding environment and camera/audio beforehand, and keep water nearby. • Prepare a few honest, specific questions about the team or role for when you are inevitably asked "do you have any questions for us?" There is no shortcut past typing the code out and getting stuck on the practice problems -- the stuck feeling is

trace it by hand, then practice under real conditions. There is no shortcut past typing the code out and getting stuck on the practice problems -- the stuck feeling is not a sign you are doing it wrong, it is the mechanism by which the pattern actually becomes yours. Work through it in order, and by Chapter 19's mixed-practice weeks, problems that would have looked unfamiliar at the start will start resolving into patterns you already recognize on sight. That recognition, built up one chapter at a time, is what "placement-ready" actually means. DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 84
